text: The surgeon performs an apical lymph node dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery laparoscopically, with the IMA pedicle dissected medially up to its origin.

The surgeon skeletonizes the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) laparoscopically, carefully exposing the vessel to facilitate potential ligation and division, ensuring control in case of bleeding.

The surgeon uses an energy device to divide the proximal extent of the dissection, entering the plane between the vascular sheath and the tunica adventitia. Crossing vessels are cauterized with diathermy.
The surgeon carefully dissects distally to identify the left colic artery, transecting the surrounding fat to facilitate the dissection. The superior rectal artery is isolated for later transection.

The inferior mesenteric vein is visible, indicating the boundary of the dissection along the mesentery. The surgeon proceeds parallel to the proximal inferior mesenteric artery, reaching the previously prepared proximal extent of the dissection.

The surgeon transects the specimen at the current level and removes it for separate pathological evaluation. The anatomy is clearly visible, including the superior rectal artery, proximal inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), left colic artery, and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) behind. The superior rectal artery is then divided, completing the final specimen preparation.

In this patient, the surgeon identifies a sigmoidal branch from the left colic artery, known as the descending branch. The anatomy is carefully examined to avoid unnecessary bleeding or compromising the left colic artery during dissection.

The surgeon divides the superior rectal artery, revealing the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The distance between the aorta and the left colic artery is measured using a silk tie. The inferior mesenteric vein is then divided.

Using a robotic system, the surgeon begins with a medial approach to the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), carefully dissecting its origin. This initial dissection allows for control of any potential bleeding at this level.
The surgeon performs a planar dissection between the vascular sheath and adventitia, carefully avoiding grasping the vessel to prevent bleeding. The vessels are controlled using bursts of diathermy.
The surgeon carefully dissects distally along the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) to identify the left colic artery. Upon identification, the surrounding fat is transected at this level.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the level of the left colic artery, ensuring not to injure any sigmoidal branches or the inferior mesenteric vein. The dissection continues until the inferior mesenteric vein is visually identified.
The surgeon safely transects tissue at a level where no vessels are expected, marking the limit of dissection with the visible inferior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon proceeds parallel to the proximal inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) until reaching the previously identified proximal extent of dissection. Care is taken to avoid injuring the more proximal inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The specimen is then transected for separate evaluation.
The surgeon demonstrates the anatomy by holding the inferior mesenteric vein while dividing the superior rectal artery. The left colic artery is visibly preserved, and the final specimen is shown.
